1: Chapter 29 contains Jeremiah's letter to the Babylonian exiles — one of the most pastorally significant documents in the Hebrew Bible. These are the words of the letter that Jeremiah the prophet sent from Jerusalem to the surviving elders of the exiles, and to the priests, the prophets, and all the people, whom Nebuchadnezzar had taken into exile from Jerusalem to Babylon. The letter was sent to those deported in 597 BC — the first exile that included Jehoiachin, Ezekiel, and the skilled artisans. The recipients include the community leadership (elders, priests, prophets) and the general population.
2: This was after King Jeconiah and the queen mother, the eunuchs, the officials of Judah and Jerusalem, the craftsmen, and the metal workers had departed from Jerusalem. The list of the 597 deportation matches 2 Kings 24:14–16: king, court, craftsmen, metal workers. The inclusion of craftsmen and metal workers is historically significant — these were the people who could manufacture weapons and military equipment. Babylon's removal of them was strategic: it left Jerusalem militarily weakened. Their absence from Jerusalem is part of the political-military context Jeremiah is addressing.
3: The letter was sent by the hand of Elasah the son of Shaphan and Gemariah the son of Hilkiah, whom Zedekiah king of Judah sent to Babylon to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon. The letter traveled with official royal messengers — Zedekiah was sending a delegation to Nebuchadnezzar, and Jeremiah sent his letter through their hands. Elasah ben Shaphan is from the Shaphan family that has been consistently supportive of Jeremiah (cf. Ahikam ben Shaphan, Jeremiah 26:24). Gemariah ben Hilkiah — possibly the son of the high priest who found the law-scroll in Josiah's reign (2 Kings 22:8). These are trustworthy carriers from reform-sympathetic families.
4: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel, to all the exiles whom I have sent into exile from Jerusalem to Babylon. The divine authorship is asserted immediately: whom I have sent into exile (asher hig'alti). YHWH sent the exiles, not merely permitted the Babylonians to deport them. The theological framing of the exile as YHWH's purposive act — not just a military defeat — is the foundation of everything that follows. If YHWH sent them, he is with them in Babylon; he can be sought there; he can restore them from there.
5: Build houses and live in them; plant gardens and eat their produce. The counterintuitive pastoral counsel begins: put down roots. The natural response to involuntary exile — maintaining refugee mentality, refusing to invest in the host land, preserving resources for the expected quick return — is exactly what Hananiah's false prophecy was encouraging. Jeremiah's counsel is the opposite: build, plant, eat. Long-term investment in the place of exile is YHWH's instruction, not compromise or disloyalty.
6: Take wives and have sons and daughters; take wives for your sons, and give your daughters in marriage, that they may bear sons and daughters; multiply there, and do not decrease. The creation mandate (be fruitful and multiply, Genesis 1:28) is applied to the Babylonian exile community: perpetuate the covenant people in the diaspora. The instruction to arrange marriages for children implies a multi-generational commitment: this is not a two-year waiting period (contra Hananiah) but an extended sojourn that will require the next generation to be established. Do not decrease: maintaining population is a form of covenant faithfulness.
7: But seek the welfare of the city where I have sent you into exile, and pray to YHWH on its behalf, for in its welfare you will find your welfare. The most theologically provocative verse in the letter: seek the shalom of the city (dirshu et-shalom ha'ir). Shalom encompasses peace, welfare, prosperity, wholeness — the comprehensive flourishing of Babylonian society. The covenant community sent into exile is instructed not merely to tolerate Babylon but to actively seek its welfare through prayer and civic engagement. The theological grounding: the exiles' welfare is bound up with Babylon's welfare. This is the foundation for the diaspora theology that shaped post-exilic Judaism and, through it, the NT understanding of the church as sojourners who pray for and serve the civil communities where God has placed them (1 Timothy 2:1–2; 1 Peter 2:11–17).
8: For thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Do not let your prophets and your diviners who are among you deceive you, and do not listen to the dreams that they dream. The exile community has its own false prophets (cf. Ahab and Zedekiah, vv21–23; Shemaiah, vv24–32) who are encouraging the same false hope Hananiah promoted in Jerusalem: quick return, no need to settle in. The warning against exilic false prophecy is the same as the warning to Jerusalem: prophets who promise what you want to hear rather than what YHWH has actually said are deceiving you.
9: For it is a lie that they are prophesying to you in my name; I did not send them, declares YHWH. The I did not send criterion applies as much in Babylon as in Jerusalem. YHWH's authentication standards are not geographically variable — the same principle that distinguishes true from false prophecy in the Temple court applies equally in the exile community. Distance from the land does not suspend covenant obligations or prophetic accountability.
10: For thus says YHWH: When seventy years are completed for Babylon, I will visit you, and I will fulfill to you my promise and bring you back to this place. The 70-year oracle from Jeremiah 25:11–12 is now addressed directly to the exiles. I will visit you (afqod etkhem) — the divine visitation-for-good that reverses the visitation-for-judgment (cf. Jeremiah 1:10 — I pluck up and destroy; Jeremiah 31:28 — I build and plant). Fulfill my promise (aqqim et-devari hatov): the good word YHWH has spoken about restoration will be executed, not merely announced. The promise is a commitment, not merely an aspiration.
11: For I know the plans I have for you, declares YHWH, plans for welfare and not for evil, to give you a future and a hope. Perhaps the most well-known verse in Jeremiah. I know the plans I have for you (ki anochi yada'ti et-hamachashavot asher anochi choshev aleikhem): the divine intentionality toward the covenant community is personal, deliberate, and forward-looking. Welfare and not evil (shalom velo lera'ah): YHWH's ultimate purpose for his people is shalom — the comprehensive flourishing of the covenant community. Future and hope (acharit vetiqvah): the exile that appears to be an ending is actually a pivot toward a future that YHWH has designed. The verse is set in the specific historical context of Babylonian exile, but its principle — divine intentionality toward covenant flourishing — has been appropriated across all contexts of disorientation and suffering.
12: Then you will call upon me and come and pray to me, and I will hear you. The restoration of prayer access — which had been withdrawn from Jerusalem (Jeremiah 7:16; Jeremiah 11:14; Jeremiah 14:11) — is promised for the exile community. When the 70 years are completed and YHWH visits them, prayer will once again function as a genuine channel of covenant communication. The hearing of prayer (shama'ti) is the restoration of the broken covenant relationship.
13: You will seek me and find me, when you seek me with all your heart. The seeking-and-finding promise is one of the most important restoration statements in the prophets. With all your heart (bechol-levavchem): the undivided heart of Deuteronomy 6:5 (love YHWH with all your heart) is the condition for finding YHWH in exile. The exile has stripped away the Temple, the land, the political structures — what remains is the heart-orientation toward YHWH that was always the core of the covenant. The exile's function in Jeremiah's theology is precisely to reduce Israel to this core.
14: I will be found by you, declares YHWH, and I will restore your fortunes and gather you from all the nations and all the places where I have driven you, declares YHWH, and I will bring you back to the place from which I sent you into exile. The restoration is comprehensive and personal: YHWH will be found — the seeker will discover that YHWH was present all along. The gathering from all nations and all places anticipates a diaspora broader than the Babylonian exile — a worldwide scattering from which YHWH will gather. The NT reads this as the gathering of the church from all nations (Matthew 24:31; John 11:52; Ephesians 2:11–22).
15: Because you have said, YHWH has raised up prophets for us in Babylon. The exiles are appealing to their own prophetic tradition in Babylon to counter Jeremiah's letter. The false prophets among the exiles were claiming YHWH's authorization — YHWH has raised up prophets for us — in support of their restoration-within-two-years message. Jeremiah must address this specific claim: having prophets is not itself proof of divine authorization.
16: Thus says YHWH concerning the king who sits on the throne of David, and concerning all the people who dwell in this city, your kinsmen who did not go out with you into exile. Jeremiah addresses the relative situations of the exiles and the Jerusalem remnant. The exiles might be tempted to think that those left in Jerusalem are better off — still in the covenant land, still in the city, still near the Temple. Jeremiah's oracle reverses this assessment.
17: Thus says YHWH of hosts: Behold, I am sending on them sword, famine, and pestilence, and I will make them like vile figs that are so bad they cannot be eaten. The bad-fig oracle of chapter 24 is restated: those who remained in Jerusalem (and will remain through the coming decade until Jerusalem's fall in 587) face the judgment triad. The vile figs that cannot be eaten image the complete worthlessness of those who evaded YHWH's appointed exile. The exiles are the good figs of chapter 24; those in Jerusalem are the bad figs.
18: I will pursue them with sword, famine, and pestilence, and will make them a horror to all the kingdoms of the earth, to be a curse, a terror, a hissing, and a reproach among all the nations where I shall drive them. The shame-catalogue for the bad-fig population (cf. Jeremiah 24:9): horror, curse, terror, hissing, reproach. Every form of public disgrace in the ancient honor-shame framework will fall on those who remain in Jerusalem and resist YHWH's judgment. The pursuit of the judgment triad is relentless — there is no escaping into Egypt or any other refuge.
19: Because they did not listen to my words, declares YHWH, that I persistently sent to them by my servants the prophets, but you would not listen, declares YHWH. The covenant audit: persistent sending, persistent non-listening. The prophetic tradition has been consistent and urgent; the people's response has been consistent and deaf. The formula summarizes the entire history of covenant communication that chapter 25 catalogued — 23 years of Jeremiah's ministry plus the full tradition of prophets before him.
20: Hear the word of YHWH, all you exiles whom I sent away from Jerusalem to Babylon. Jeremiah refocuses on the exiles as his primary audience after the digression addressing the Jerusalem situation. The exiles need to receive this word to navigate their situation wisely — understanding that their present condition is YHWH's purposive act, that they have a genuine future, and that the false prophets among them are dangerous.
21: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel, concerning Ahab the son of Kolaiah and Zedekiah the son of Maaseiah, who are prophesying a lie to you in my name: Behold, I will deliver them into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and he shall strike them down before your eyes. Two specific false prophets in Babylon are named and condemned: Ahab ben Kolaiah and Zedekiah ben Maaseiah. Their fate is judicial execution by Nebuchadnezzar — not martyrdom for their faith but punishment as political troublemakers whose false prophecy destabilized the exile community.
22: Because of them this curse shall be used by all the exiles from Judah in Babylon: YHWH make you like Zedekiah and Ahab, whom the king of Babylon roasted in the fire. The executions were by fire — a Babylonian method of capital punishment documented in the ancient sources. The names become a curse formula: YHWH make you like Zedekiah and Ahab — their fate so terrible that it becomes the template for invocation of maximum punishment. Daniel 3 provides the background: Babylonian execution by fire was a known institution. These two false prophets died the death they implicitly invited on others by their false prophecy.
23: Because they have done an outrageous thing in Israel: they have committed adultery with their neighbors' wives, and they have spoken in my name lying words that I did not command them. I am the one who knows, and I am witness, declares YHWH. The double indictment: sexual immorality (literal adultery) and prophetic fraud (lying in YHWH's name). The combination echoes chapter 23's indictment of Jerusalem's prophets who commit adultery and walk in lies (Jeremiah 23:14). The false prophets of Babylon have replicated the corruption of Jerusalem's prophetic establishment in the exile community. I am witness (ani eid): YHWH is the self-declaring divine witness — sufficient testimony without human corroboration.
24: To Shemaiah of Nehelam you shall say. A third false prophet in Babylon is addressed — Shemaiah of Nehelam (the location is unknown but possibly a family or place designation). Shemaiah had written letters to Jerusalem attempting to have Jeremiah arrested, and the narrative of his case and Jeremiah's counter-oracle runs through vv25–32.
25: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: You have sent letters in your name to all the people who are in Jerusalem, and to Zephaniah the son of Maaseiah the priest, and to all the priests, saying. Shemaiah's letters were sent to the Jerusalem religious authorities, specifically to Zephaniah the priest (who appears in 21:1 and 37:3 as part of the delegations sent to Jeremiah). The letter attempted to invoke the authority of the Temple establishment to suppress Jeremiah's letter.
26: YHWH has made you priest instead of Jehoiada the priest, to have charge in the house of YHWH over every madman who prophesies, to put him in the stocks and neck irons. Shemaiah's letter argued that the priest had jurisdiction over those who claimed to be prophets but behaved like madmen. The word for madman (meshugga) — the same root used by Elisha's servant (2 Kings 9:11: this madman came to you) — was applied to those who behaved with prophetic ecstasy. Shemaiah is arguing that Jeremiah's behavior falls within this category and that the Temple authorities should restrain him.
27: Now why have you not rebuked Jeremiah of Anathoth who is prophesying to you? Shemaiah's rhetorical question to Zephaniah the priest: why are you allowing Jeremiah to continue? The fact that this question could be sent suggests that Shemaiah was aware of Jeremiah's letter to the exiles and found it dangerously subversive — counseling submission to Babylon and long-term investment in exile was politically destabilizing to the exile community's hope for quick return.
28: For he has sent to us in Babylon, saying, Your exile will be long; build houses and live in them, and plant gardens and eat their produce. Shemaiah quotes Jeremiah's counsel as evidence of its dangerous quality: your exile will be long. This is exactly what Jeremiah said — but Shemaiah presents it as proof of Jeremiah's false and harmful prophecy. The contested interpretation of the exile's duration is the central dispute: Hananiah said two years; Shemaiah agrees with Hananiah; Jeremiah says 70 years.
29: Zephaniah the priest read this letter in the hearing of Jeremiah the prophet. Rather than acting on Shemaiah's demand to arrest Jeremiah, Zephaniah read him the letter — the same kind of support Ahikam had provided at the Temple-sermon trial (Jeremiah 26:24). The reform-sympathetic strand within the priesthood (traceable back to the Shaphan family) continues to protect Jeremiah from institutional suppression. Zephaniah will later consult Jeremiah on behalf of Zedekiah (Jeremiah 37:3; Jeremiah 52:24).
30: Then the word of YHWH came to Jeremiah: the divine response to Shemaiah's letter. The pattern is consistent: Jeremiah receives divine authorization for his counter-oracles through the normal prophetic channel. He does not respond impulsively to the letter but waits for the divine word.
31: Send to all the exiles, saying, Thus says YHWH concerning Shemaiah of Nehelam: Because Shemaiah prophesied to you when I did not send him, and made you trust in a lie. The I did not send verdict is issued against Shemaiah — the same verdict against Hananiah (Jeremiah 28:15) and the false prophets generally (Jeremiah 23:21). The harm specified: made you trust in a lie — exactly the charge against Hananiah (Jeremiah 28:15). The false prophets' sin is consistently the same: using YHWH's name to create false security that prevents the repentance and reorientation that would actually bring life.
32: Therefore thus says YHWH: Behold, I will punish Shemaiah of Nehelam and his offspring. He shall not have anyone living among this people, and he shall not see the good that I will do to my people, declares YHWH, for he has spoken rebellion against YHWH. Shemaiah's punishment is exclusion from the restoration: he will have no one living among the covenant community and will not see the good that YHWH does for his people in the end. This is the harshest possible prophetic punishment — not physical death but covenant exclusion. The false prophet who promised quick return will not participate in the actual return. His offspring — the extension of his legacy — will share the exclusion.
